msItem.title: Calendar, in Latin with some English elements (fol. 2r)

msItem.note: De baptismo. Not separated from previous item in the MS.

msItem.rubric: Forma ad absoluendum quemcumque per bullam concessam de plena remissione peccatoruem semel in articulo mortis

msItem.note: De extrema unctione ; fols. 36v-37r contain an insertion in the text, referring to Archbishop Pecham’s Lambeth Council of 1281

Gives full Psalm texts and not just incipits. The explicit on fol. 54v states ‘Numquam dicitur ali[a] commendatio in ecclesia sarisburiensis quam ista predicta, sive corpus [presens] fuit, sive non’).

msItem.rubric: With prayers and readings added; Pro fratribus et sororibus, In die nativitatis domini ad missam, de sancto stephani officium and so on through the Massacre of the Holy Innocents, Presentation at the Temple, Epiphany, Candlemass, Annunciation. Includes rite for In dedicacione ecclesie ad missam officium, but no clues to any particular location.

msItem.note: With musical notation (the sung elements of the mass), starting with Christmas
Canon of the Mass, followed by readings for major feasts and variations for particular intentions (Missa per fratribus et sororibus, pro salute amici, pro infirmo, pro defunctis anniversariis)
With wide range of prayers (orationes) for different ‘types’ of deceased (a religious, friend, benefactor, parent).

msItem.rubric: Forma ad absoluendum quemcumque per bullam concessam de plena remissionem peccatorem semel in articulo mortis audita confessione in iuncta sibi alique penitentia infirmus si potest
msItem.note: Includes suffrages to Wulfstan of Worcester, Richard of Chichester, Edward King and Confessor, Edmund.

msItem.rubric: Ordo ad anachoritam includendum secundum usum Sarisburiensis
Ordo ad purificandum mulierum pst partum

msItem.note: For various occasions: fol. 162r-v, Benedictio nove domus; fol. 162v, Benedictio quocumque volueris; fols. 162v-163r, Benedictio ensis novi militis; fols. 163r-166v, Benedictio scutum et baculum ad duellum; fols. 166v-167r, Benedictio seminis; fol. 167r-v, Benedictio pomorum; fols. 167v-168r, Benedictio oculorum infirmorum; fol. 168r-v, Benedictio navis.

Saint Gregory’s Trental: Ordo tricemalis sancti Gregorii pro liberacione anime matris sue in penis purgatoriis talis est (a ‘trental’ to be offered up for the dead, spread out over the liturgical year on major feast days, which are listed here by their sequences (Introits); see Pfaff, ‘The English Devotion of St. Gregory’s Trental’).

Prayers: Deus cui proprium est miserere semper; Omnipotens sempiterne deus qui facis mirabilia; Deus qui caritatis dona; Deus a quo sancta desideria; Ineffabilem ?misteriam tuam; Fidelium deus omnium conditor; Pietate tua quesumus domine.

A further litany including two saints usually indicative of strong Winchester connections. They are Iudoc, a 7th century hermit whose body was found incorrupt at St. Josse near Étaples and was transferred to the New Minster at Winchester in 902, and Grimbald, who had Iudoc’s remains enshrined in the new church. (Grimbald was Dean of Winchester and had assisted Alfred the Great in his translations). Taken together, these two saints are a clear sign of Winchester influence in the making of this book. Note that this Litany also includes Wandregresille/Wandrille, more usually found in French MSS (is she connected to Iudoc???), and Frideswide and Etheldreda whose feasts were not officially promulgated until 1480 (Pfaff, New Feasts, p. 3)

supportDesc.collation: Based on contemporary catchwords and quire markings: I 6, II-X 8, XI 8+1 [5 a single leaf insertion, miniature, fol. 83], XII-XXIV 8, XXV 8 [8 cancelled, stub visible]. Catchwords are written horizontally in a current hand in the inner lower margin; quire markings begin in quire II (ie after the Calendar), with a †, and then run from ‘a’ through to ‘z’ (no ‘i’, ‘u’ or ‘w’).

layoutDesc.layout: fols 7r-198v, text block 155 105 , ruled 26 lines in brown ink, written below top line; fols 7r-13r, long lines, thereafter in two columns.
fols 1r-6b, written space 165 120 ; ruled 34 lines in black ink, all pricking still visible.

handDesc.handNote: Hand 1, fols 1r-6v (Calendar): gothic bookhand with some anglicana features; these become more prominent in the English additions. Red and black ink

Hand 2, fols 7r-198v, except fols 139v-140r: competent semi-quadrata hand with fine oblique seriphs and barbed ascenders on some letters. Writes main texts and texts set to music. Writes a simple ink cross and leaves space for more elaborate cross to be added later (direction for priest to make the sign of the cross).
Hand 3, fols 139v-140r: lighter ink and more formal quadrata aspect than Hand 2; possibly Hand 2 trying out a more formal liturgical script?

musicNotation.p: Notation on staves of four lines.

decoDesc.decoNote: fol. 83v: full page miniature on added leaf, Crucixion with Mary and John at foot of cross, two thieves depicted with souls departing, one to heaven and one to hell; skulls and bones litter the grass foreground, background in red with scrolling gold decoration.
Major initials with borders: fol. 7r, fol. 84r, fol. 130v; calendar KL initials forming borders.

binding.p: Inboard binding, English, contemporary and mid-18th century. Sewn on 4 thick double alum-tawed supports (original). Original endbands, primary sewing worked in plain thread with no front bead over alum tawed cores, slips laced into boards. Remains of secondary sewing in coloured silk incorporating original alum-tawed skin covering. Oak boards, with heavy external perimeter cushion taken right down the inner edge, internal bevel on spine edge. There is a large round hole (18mm diameter) drilled slightly above centre in the back board. Covering, 18th-century quarter tanned brown calf with Dutch combed marbled paper on the sides. A pair of strap recesses on the foredge of the front board, which retain the stubs of the straps, and a central strap recess also on the foredge of front board, now empty. Tangs of two copper alloy sidepins showing through back pastedown, relating to the pair of straps on the front board. It is impossible to say whether the single or double clasps were the earlier.

history.provenance: a) fols 1r, 48v-49r, 62v: pen trials in a child’s hand? b) Calendar, June, s.xv hand: sancti petroci c) fol. 35v, upper margin: Ann Kirkham her hand and penn Amen 1698; fol. 37v, upper margin: Ann. Ann Kirkham ffor d) fol. 49r, lower margin, John Tucker his hand e) fol. 135r, Madam Adam Satt. Hood nere Totnes.
Offered for sale, Christie's, 20 Dec. 1972, as part of lots 204, 206-211, but withdrawn before the sale.
Sold to the National Trust in 1991.
history.acquisition: Deposited by the National Trust in 2026.

recordHist.source: Abbreviated from a draft description by Suzanne Reynolds.
